---
title: "Population and Demography"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
bibliography: references.bib
csl: nature.csl
link-citations: true
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Population and Demography}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

## Population projections

`site_file$population`

Site-level population distributions are derived at pixel level from WorldPop @worldpop. For 2000 to 2014 we use the “Unconstrained individual countries 2000 to 2020 UN adjusted (1 km resolution)” rasters (alias: wpicuadj1km). For 2015 to 2030 we use the “Individual countries 2015 to 2030 (1 km resolution) R2025A v1” rasters (alias: G2_CN_POP_R25A_1km).

For each year, pixel values are re-scaled so that the raster sum matches UN WPP country-level population totals @UNWPP.

We provide population either by 1-year age bands (`site_file$population$population_by_age`) or as a total (`site_file$population$population_total`).

### Population at risk

`site_file$population$par_pf`
`site_file$population$par_pv`
`site_file$population$par`

Population at risk for Plasmodium falciparum, Plasmodium vivax, or either species is estimated by masking the total population raster using areas with active transmission (prevalence > 0%) in year 2000 @MAP.

### Urbanisation

We use UN projections of national urbanisation levels @UNWUP to estimate the proportion of the population living in urban areas each year. Pixels are assigned to the “urban” class in descending order of population density until the implied national urban population total for that year is reached. Remaining pixels are classified as rural.

## Demography

Population age structure over time is based on UN WPP @UNWPP and is accessible via the age-stratified population (`site_file$population$population_by_age`).

Model-input demography (`site_file$population$demography`) is adjusted because the model population size is constant over time. This adjustment preserves the time-varying age structure implied by UN WPP within a constant-size model population, and is performed using the [peeps R package](https://github.com/mrc-ide/peeps).

Model-input mortality rates are specified for neonates (0 to 30 days), young infants (31 days to 1 year), older infants (1 to 5 years), and then in five-year age bands.

# Citations
